codeSnapshot.label: f1_score.py
codeSnapshot.code:
import numpy as np
from sklearn.metrics import f1_score, fbeta_score, precision_score, recall_score

y_true = np.array([1,1,1,1,0,0,0,0,1,0])
y_pred = np.array([1,0,1,1,0,0,1,0,1,1])  # TP=4, FP=2, FN=1, TN=3

# F1 = 2·P·R/(P+R) = 2·TP / (2·TP + FP + FN)
precision = precision_score(y_true, y_pred)  # 4/(4+2) = 0.667
recall    = recall_score(y_true, y_pred)     # 4/(4+1) = 0.800
f1        = f1_score(y_true, y_pred)         # 2*0.667*0.800/(0.667+0.800) = 0.727
print(f'P={precision:.3f}, R={recall:.3f}, F1={f1:.3f}')

# Fβ: β < 1 weights precision more; β > 1 weights recall more
# F0.5: emphasise precision (e.g. information retrieval — don't want false alarms)
f05 = fbeta_score(y_true, y_pred, beta=0.5)
# F2: emphasise recall (e.g. cancer screening — don't miss positives)
f2  = fbeta_score(y_true, y_pred, beta=2.0)
print(f'F0.5={f05:.3f}, F2={f2:.3f}')

# Multi-class F1 averaging strategies
y_true_mc = [0,1,2,0,1,2,0,1,2,0,0,1]
y_pred_mc = [0,1,1,0,2,2,0,0,2,0,1,1]

# Macro: average F1 per class (equal class weight)
f1_macro = f1_score(y_true_mc, y_pred_mc, average='macro')
# Weighted: average F1 weighted by class support
f1_weighted = f1_score(y_true_mc, y_pred_mc, average='weighted')
# Micro: compute global TP, FP, FN then compute F1
f1_micro = f1_score(y_true_mc, y_pred_mc, average='micro')
# Per-class: F1 for each class
f1_per_class = f1_score(y_true_mc, y_pred_mc, average=None)

print(f'Macro F1={f1_macro:.3f}, Weighted F1={f1_weighted:.3f}, Micro F1={f1_micro:.3f}')
print(f'Per-class F1: {f1_per_class}')

# Why harmonic mean? Penalises imbalance between P and R
# Arithmetic mean of P=0.9, R=0.1: (0.9+0.1)/2 = 0.5 — looks OK
# Harmonic mean (F1): 2*0.9*0.1/(0.9+0.1) = 0.18 — reveals the imbalance
print(f'Arithmetic mean: {(0.9+0.1)/2:.2f}')
print(f'Harmonic mean (F1): {2*0.9*0.1/(0.9+0.1):.2f}')

eli3: F1 score is the harmonic mean of precision and recall. Precision measures 'of the things I flagged, how many were actually correct?' while recall measures 'of all the things I should have found, how many did I find?' F1 combines these into one number.

F1 is better than accuracy for imbalanced datasets: if you have 99% negative class, a model that always says negative gets 99% accuracy but 0% F1 (because it catches zero true positives — recall=0 → F1=0).

The harmonic mean punishes extreme imbalance between precision and recall. A model with precision=0.9 and recall=0.1 has F1=0.18, not 0.5 — this correctly reflects that the model is useless in practice even though precision looks good.

whenWhy.title: Imbalanced binary classification
whenWhy.body: F1 is the standard metric when classes are imbalanced and both precision and recall matter. NLP tasks (named entity recognition, relation extraction), medical diagnosis (disease detection), and information retrieval all use F1 as the primary evaluation metric. F1=0 when either precision=0 or recall=0 — it correctly penalises degenerate models that predict everything as positive (recall=1, precision=low) or never predict positive (precision=undefined, recall=0).

whenWhy.title: Fβ when errors have asymmetric costs
whenWhy.body: Fβ = (1+β²)·P·R / (β²·P+R). β=1 is standard F1 (equal weight to P and R). β<1 weights precision more: F0.5 is used in search and recommendation where false alarms (irrelevant results) are especially costly. β>1 weights recall more: F2 is used in fraud detection and medical screening where missing a positive is worse than a false alarm. The choice of β should reflect the ratio of FN cost to FP cost: β = √(FN_cost / FP_cost).

whenWhy.title: Multi-class evaluation and averaging strategy
whenWhy.body: For K-class problems, per-class F1 scores can be aggregated: Macro F1 (equal weight per class) is sensitive to rare class performance. Weighted F1 (weight by class support) is dominated by majority classes. Micro F1 (global TP/FP/FN) = accuracy in balanced settings. In machine translation and NLP benchmarks (GLUE, SuperGLUE), macro F1 is standard — it equally weights all classes including rare ones. For production systems with imbalanced classes, weighted F1 or class-specific F1 is more interpretable.

deepDiveIntro: F1 is the harmonic mean of precision and recall, computed as 2 times the number of true positives divided by the sum of two times true positives plus false positives and false negatives. The harmonic mean is always at most the arithmetic mean, with equality only when precision equals recall. It is particularly sensitive to whichever of the two is lower — if either is near zero, F1 is near zero. Van Rijsbergen (1979) introduced the F-measure in information retrieval. [1]

The Fβ variant gives a way to weight recall more or less than precision. When β is greater than 1, the formula puts more emphasis on recall; when β is less than 1, it emphasizes precision; when β equals 1 it is the standard F1. At the extremes, Fβ approaches pure recall as β grows large and approaches pure precision as β shrinks toward zero. [1]

deepDive.heading: Harmonic Mean and Why It Matters
deepDive.body: A model with perfect precision but zero recall has an arithmetic average of 0.5, which looks deceptively acceptable. F1 gives zero for that model, correctly reflecting that it catches nothing useful. The harmonic mean penalizes imbalance between precision and recall more severely than a simple average does. If either component is unacceptably low, F1 is low — that is exactly the behavior you want when both matter. [1]

deepDive.heading: Macro vs Micro vs Weighted F1
deepDive.body: For multi-class problems, there are three common ways to aggregate per-class F1 scores. Macro F1 takes a simple average across classes, giving equal weight to rare and common classes alike. Weighted F1 averages by the number of samples in each class, so larger classes dominate. Micro F1 pools all true positives, false positives, and false negatives globally before computing the score, which makes it equivalent to accuracy on balanced datasets. [1]

deepDive.heading: F1 for Span-Level NLP Evaluation
deepDive.body: In tasks like named entity recognition, a prediction counts as a true positive only if it exactly matches both the span and the label of a reference annotation. Predicted spans that do not match are false positives, and reference spans that are missed are false negatives. This exact-match standard is stricter than token-level scoring, which allows partial credit. CoNLL NER uses span-level F1; SQuAD uses token-level F1 with text normalization. [1]

youtubeLabel: F1 Score, Precision and Recall Explained
takeaways: F1 = 2·P·R/(P+R) = 2·TP/(2·TP+FP+FN) — harmonic mean of precision and recall. [1]
F1=0 if either P=0 or R=0 — correctly penalises degenerate classifiers. [1]
Fβ: β<1 favours precision (search), β>1 favours recall (screening), β=1 is balanced F1. [1]
Macro F1: equal class weight; Micro F1: global TP/FP/FN; Weighted: by support. [1]
Use F1 over accuracy for imbalanced classification — accuracy can be high with 0% recall. [1]

edgeCases.q: Why does the F1 score underperform the Matthews Correlation Coefficient for severely imbalanced datasets?
edgeCases.a: F1 only considers TP, FP, and FN — it completely ignores TN. In severely imbalanced datasets (e.g., 99% negative), a model that predicts all as positive has recall=1.0, precision=0.01, F1=0.02 — low but not capturing the model's failure to identify true negatives. MCC accounts for all four cells (TP, FP, FN, TN). A model that predicts all positive gets MCC = (TP·TN-FP·FN)/√(...) where TN=0 → MCC near 0 (correct — model is no better than guessing all positive). Chicco & Jurman (2020) show that MCC is more informative than F1 across 30 binary classification benchmark datasets, particularly when TN information is significant. For reporting, prefer MCC when TN is meaningful; F1 when TN is not (information retrieval where there are infinite potential true negatives).

edgeCases.q: How should you set β in Fβ score in practice?
edgeCases.a: β should reflect the relative cost of false negatives vs false positives: β = √(FN_cost / FP_cost). If a false negative costs 4× a false positive: β = √4 = 2 (use F2 — weights recall twice as much as precision). If a false positive costs 4× a false negative: β = √(1/4) = 0.5 (use F0.5). In practice: elicit the cost ratio from domain experts or business stakeholders. If they can't specify costs: use F1 (equal costs). For medical screening: FN_cost >> FP_cost (missing disease is worse than unnecessary follow-up) → β > 1. For spam filtering: FP_cost > FN_cost (blocking legitimate email is worse than missing spam) → β < 1.

sources.label: Powers (2011) — Evaluation: From Precision, Recall and F-Measure to ROC, Informedness, Markedness & Correlation, JMLR
